      { text: "<em>Will you be using</em> the car tonight?", note: '(polite question)' },

      { text: "By 10 o'clock, we <em>will have finished</em> dinner." },

      { text: "By the end of the year, she <em>will have lived</em> here for a decade." }

    ],

    compare: [

      { col1: 'Will be doing', col2: 'Will have done' },

      { col1: 'In progress at a point', col2: 'Completed before a point' },

      { col1: "I'll be sleeping at midnight.", col2: "I'll have finished by midnight." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 25, cat: 'future',

    title: 'When I do / When I\'ve done — When and if',

    subtitle: 'Future time clauses',

    rules: [

      'After <em>when, while, before, after, as soon as, until/till, by the time</em> — use <strong>present</strong> (not will).',

      'You can also use <strong>present perfect</strong> after these conjunctions.',

      '"When" = certain event. "If" = possible event.'

    ],

    formula: 'When I arrive (NOT when I will arrive), I\'ll call you.\nIf = possible. When = certain.',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>When you arrive</em>, give me a call. (not: will arrive)" },

      { text: "We'll go out <em>when it stops</em> raining." },

      { text: "<em>After I've finished</em> this chapter, I'll take a break." },

      { text: "<em>If</em> it rains, we'll cancel the picnic. (possible)" },

      { text: "<em>When</em> I retire, I'll travel. (certain plan)" }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "When I will see him, I'll tell him.", right: "When I see him, I'll tell him." }

    ]

  },

  // ── MODALS ───────────────────────────────────────────────────

  {

    id: 26, cat: 'modals',

    title: 'Can, could and (be) able to',

    subtitle: 'Ability and possibility',

    rules: [

      '"Can" = ability/possibility in the <strong>present</strong>.',

      '"Could" = ability in the <strong>past</strong> OR theoretical possibility.',

      '"Be able to" = can be used in all tenses (will be able to, have been able to).',

      'For a specific past achievement, use "managed to" or "was able to" (not "could").'

    ],

    formula: 'can/could + base verb\nbe (able) to + base verb (all tenses)',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>Can you swim</em>? / <em>Could you swim</em> when you were five?" },

      { text: "I couldn't sleep last night." },

      { text: "After months of practice, she <em>was able to</em> run 10km." },

      { text: "In the future, we <em>will be able to</em> travel to Mars." },

      { text: "The word 'dream' <em>can be</em> a noun or a verb." }

    ],

    notes: ['"Could have done" = had the ability but didn\'t use it: "I could have helped but I didn\'t."'],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "I could pass the exam last year.", right: "I was able to / managed to pass the exam last year. (specific success)" }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 27, cat: 'modals',

    title: 'Could (do) and could have (done)',

    subtitle: 'Past ability and past possibility',

    rules: [

      '"Could do" = theoretical possibility now / polite request.',

      '"Could have done" = <strong>was possible but didn\'t happen</strong>.',

      '"Couldn\'t have done" = it was impossible.'

    ],

    formula: 'could + base verb → present possibility/request\ncould have + past participle → past possibility (unrealised)',

    examples: [

      { text: "You <em>could be</em> right. (it's possible you are)" },

      { text: "I <em>could have gone</em> to the party, but I stayed home." },

      { text: "That was dangerous! You <em>could have been</em> killed." },

      { text: "I <em>couldn't have done</em> it without your help." }

    ],

    compare: [

      { col1: '"could do"', col2: '"could have done"' },

      { col1: 'Possible now', col2: 'Was possible (past, unrealised)' },

      { col1: "You could call her.", col2: "You could have called her." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 28, cat: 'modals',

    title: 'Must and can\'t',

    subtitle: 'Logical deductions',

    rules: [

      '"Must" = <strong>logical deduction</strong> — I\'m sure this is true.',

      '"Can\'t" = <strong>logical deduction</strong> — I\'m sure this is NOT true.',

      'These are NOT about obligation — see Unit 31 for must = obligation.'

    ],

    formula: 'must + base verb → almost certain it\'s true\ncan\'t + base verb → almost certain it\'s NOT true',

    examples: [

      { text: "You've been travelling all day. You <em>must be</em> tired." },

      { text: "He <em>must have</em> forgotten. (logical deduction about past)" },

      { text: "That <em>can't be</em> Tom — he's in Paris." },

      { text: "She passed every exam. She <em>must study</em> very hard." }

    ],

    compare: [

      { col1: '"must" (deduction)', col2: '"can\'t" (deduction)' },

      { col1: 'I\'m sure it IS true', col2: "I'm sure it's NOT true" },

      { col1: "She must be home.", col2: "She can't be home." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 29, cat: 'modals',

    title: 'May and might 1',

    subtitle: 'Possibility',

    rules: [

      '"May/might" = <strong>possible but not certain</strong>.',

      '"May not / might not" = possible that it won\'t happen.',

      '"May/might" have roughly the same meaning; "might" is slightly less certain.',

      'Use <em>may/might be + -ing</em> for possible future action in progress.'

    ],

    formula: 'may/might + base verb → possible (present/future)\nmay/might have + past participle → possible (past)',

    examples: [

      { text: "I <em>may go</em> to Paris next week — not sure yet." },

      { text: "<em>It might rain</em> this afternoon. Take an umbrella." },

      { text: "She <em>may not come</em> to the meeting." },

      { text: "<em>He might have missed</em> the train." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "It mights rain.", right: "It might rain." },

      { wrong: "He may to be late.", right: "He may be late." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 30, cat: 'modals',

    title: 'May and might 2',

    subtitle: '"Might as well" and suggestions',

    rules: [

      '"Might as well" = there\'s no better option, so you may as well do it.',

      '"Could" = suggestion (You could try calling him).',

      '"May/might as well" is often used when you\'re not very enthusiastic.'

    ],

    formula: 'might as well + base verb → no better option available',

    examples: [

      { text: "We've finished early. We <em>might as well</em> go home." },

      { text: "There\'s nothing on TV. I <em>might as well</em> go to bed." },

      { text: "You <em>could try</em> phoning — she might be there." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 31, cat: 'modals',

    title: 'Have to and must',

    subtitle: 'Obligation and necessity',

    rules: [

      '"Must" = obligation from the <strong>speaker</strong>. "Have to" = external obligation.',

      'In practice, both often mean the same.',

      'For past obligation, use <strong>had to</strong> (not "must").',

      '"Don\'t have to" = NOT necessary. "Mustn\'t" = NOT allowed (different meanings!).'

    ],

    formula: 'must → personal obligation / strong recommendation\nhave to → external rule / necessity',

    examples: [

      { text: "You <em>must see</em> this film — it's incredible. (personal recommendation)" },

      { text: "I <em>have to be</em> at work by 9. (external rule)" },

      { text: "She <em>had to wait</em> an hour for the bus. (past)" },

      { text: "You <em>don't have to</em> wear a tie. (not necessary)" },

      { text: "You <em>mustn't</em> tell anyone. (not allowed)" }

    ],

    compare: [

      { col1: '"Don\'t have to"', col2: '"Mustn\'t"' },

      { col1: 'Not necessary', col2: 'Not allowed / forbidden' },

      { col1: "You don't have to come.", col2: "You mustn't be late." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 32, cat: 'modals',

    title: 'Must / mustn\'t / needn\'t',

    subtitle: 'Obligation, prohibition, and absence of necessity',

    rules: [

      '"Must" = obligation / strong recommendation.',

      '"Mustn\'t" = prohibition (it\'s forbidden).',

      '"Needn\'t" = it\'s not necessary (you have a choice).',

      '"Need" can be used as a modal (needn\'t) or as a regular verb (don\'t need to).'

    ],

    formula: 'must → do it / needn\'t → not necessary / mustn\'t → don\'t do it',

    examples: [

      { text: "You <em>must</em> stop at red lights." },

      { text: "You <em>mustn't</em> park here." },

      { text: "You <em>needn't shout</em> — I can hear you." },

      { text: "<em>Needn't have done</em> = did it but it was unnecessary: 'You needn't have cooked — we were going out.'" }

    ],

    compare: [

      { col1: '"Must"', col2: '"Mustn\'t"', col3: '"Needn\'t"' },

      { col1: 'Required', col2: 'Forbidden', col3: 'Not required' },

      { col1: 'Do it.', col2: "Don't do it.", col3: "You don't have to do it." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 33, cat: 'modals',

    title: 'Should 1',

    subtitle: 'Advice and obligation',

    rules: [

      '"Should" = advice / recommendation / moral obligation.',

      '"Should have" = past advice — something would have been a good idea but wasn\'t done.',

      '"Shouldn\'t" = it\'s a bad idea / wrong.'

    ],

    formula: 'should + base verb → advice/recommendation\nshould have + past participle → past regret/criticism',

    examples: [

      { text: "You <em>should see</em> a doctor about that cough." },

      { text: "<em>Should I</em> take an umbrella?" },

      { text: "You <em>shouldn't work</em> so hard." },

      { text: "I <em>should have studied</em> more. (I didn't — I regret it)" },

      { text: "She <em>shouldn't have left</em> without saying goodbye." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "I should to call him.", right: "I should call him." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 34, cat: 'modals',

    title: 'Should 2',

    subtitle: '"Should" with surprise and after certain expressions',

    rules: [

      '"Should" after: suggest, recommend, insist, demand, order, propose — typically without "to".',

      '"Should" can express surprise or unexpectedness.',

      '"Why should...?" = expresses surprise or annoyance at something.'

    ],

    formula: 'suggest/recommend + that + subject + should + verb',

    examples: [

      { text: "I suggest that you <em>should see</em> a lawyer." },

      { text: "It's strange that she <em>should say</em> that." },

      { text: "<em>Why should</em> I apologise? It's not my fault!" },

      { text: "If you <em>should</em> see Tom, tell him to call me. (if by any chance)" }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 35, cat: 'modals',

    title: 'Had better / It\'s time...',

    subtitle: 'Urgent advice and overdue actions',

    rules: [

      '"Had better" = strong advice for a specific situation (warning of consequences).',

      '"Had better not" = strong advice NOT to do something.',

      '"It\'s time to/It\'s time you did" = something should be done now (overdue).',

      'Note: "had better" ≠ "would rather" (preference).'

    ],

    formula: 'had better + base verb (NOT had better to)\nIt\'s time + to + base verb / It\'s time + subject + past tense',

    examples: [

      { text: "You <em>had better take</em> an umbrella — it looks like rain." },

      { text: "We <em>had better not</em> be late." },

      { text: "It's 2am. <em>It's time to go</em> to bed." },

      { text: "<em>It's time you got</em> a job." }

    ],

    compare: [

      { col1: '"Should"', col2: '"Had better"' },

      { col1: 'General advice', col2: 'Specific urgent advice' },

      { col1: 'You should eat less.', col2: "You'd better eat something now." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 36, cat: 'modals',

    title: 'Would',

    subtitle: 'Willingness, habits, and politeness',

    rules: [

      '"Would" = past willingness (she wouldn\'t help us).',

      '"Would" = past habit / repeated action (when I was a child, I would...).',

      '"Would" for polite requests and offers.',

      '"Would rather" = preference.'

    ],

    formula: 'would + base verb\nwould rather + base verb (than)',

    examples: [

      { text: "She <em>wouldn't open</em> the door. (refused)" },

      { text: "When I was young, I <em>would go</em> fishing every weekend." },

      { text: "<em>Would you like</em> some coffee?" },

      { text: "I <em>would rather</em> stay home than go out." },

      { text: "I'd rather you <em>didn't</em> tell anyone." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "I would rather to stay.", right: "I would rather stay." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 37, cat: 'modals',

    title: 'Can/could/would you...? — Requests, offers, permission',

    subtitle: 'Polite language',

    rules: [

      '"Can/could you...?" = request (could is more polite).',

      '"Can/may I...?" = permission.',

      '"Would/could you...?" = formal request.',

      '"Shall I/we...?" = offer or suggestion.'

    ],

    formula: 'Request: Can/Could you + base verb?\nOffer: Shall I/Would you like...?',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>Can you</em> open the window? (informal request)" },

      { text: "<em>Could you</em> help me? (more polite)" },

      { text: "<em>May I use</em> your phone?" },

      { text: "<em>Would you like</em> a drink?" },

      { text: "<em>Shall I</em> carry that for you?" }

    ],

    compare: [

      { col1: 'Less formal', col2: 'More formal' },

      { col1: 'Can you help?', col2: 'Could you possibly help?' },

      { col1: 'Can I sit here?', col2: 'May I sit here?' }

    ]

  },

  // ── IF & WISH ────────────────────────────────────────────────

  {

    id: 38, cat: 'if-wish',

    title: 'If I do... and if I did...',

    subtitle: 'Real and unreal conditions',

    rules: [

      'Type 1 (real): <strong>If + present → will</strong>. The condition is possible.',

      'Type 2 (unreal): <strong>If + past → would</strong>. The condition is imaginary now.',

      '"If I were you, I would..." — always use "were", not "was" in formal/careful usage.',

      'Do NOT use "would" in the if-clause.'

    ],

    formula: 'Type 1: If + present simple, will/can/may + base verb\nType 2: If + past simple, would/could/might + base verb',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>If it rains</em>, we'll cancel the match. (possible)" },

      { text: "<em>If I had</em> more money, I'd travel the world. (unreal/imaginary)" },

      { text: "<em>If I were you</em>, I wouldn't go." },

      { text: "What <em>would you do if</em> you lost your job?" }

    ],

    notes: ['"If I was / If I were" — both used, but "were" is more formal. "If I were you" is a set phrase.'],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "If I would have time, I would help.", right: "If I had time, I would help." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 39, cat: 'if-wish',

    title: 'If I knew... / I wish I knew...',

    subtitle: 'Unreal present/future conditions and wishes',

    rules: [

      '"I wish + past" = I want the present to be different.',

      '"If + past simple → would" = unreal present condition.',

      '"Wish" is always followed by a past form for present/future wishes.',

      '"Wish" can also take "could" for ability wishes.'

    ],

    formula: 'I wish + subject + past tense (present wish)\nI wish I knew. / I wish I could fly.',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>I wish I knew</em> the answer. (I don't know)" },

      { text: "<em>I wish I could speak</em> better French." },

      { text: "<em>I wish you were</em> here." },

      { text: "<em>I wish it wasn't</em> so cold." },

      { text: "<em>If I knew</em> her number, I'd call her." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "I wish I know the answer.", right: "I wish I knew the answer." },

      { wrong: "I wish I can speak Spanish.", right: "I wish I could speak Spanish." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 40, cat: 'if-wish',

    title: 'If I had known... / I wish I had known...',

    subtitle: 'Unreal past conditions',

    rules: [

      'Type 3 (past unreal): <strong>If + past perfect → would have + past participle</strong>.',

      '"I wish + past perfect" = regret about the past.',

      'Do NOT use "would have" in the if-clause.',

      '"Could have" / "might have" can replace "would have" in the result clause.'

    ],

    formula: 'If + had (done), would/could/might + have (done)',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>If I had known</em> you were ill, I <em>would have visited</em> you." },

      { text: "<em>I wish I had studied</em> harder. (past regret)" },

      { text: "She <em>could have been</em> seriously hurt." },

      { text: "If the weather <em>hadn't been</em> so bad, we <em>would have gone</em> out." }

    ],

    compare: [

      { col1: 'Type 2 (unreal present)', col2: 'Type 3 (unreal past)' },

      { col1: "If I had time, I'd help.", col2: "If I'd had time, I'd have helped." },

      { col1: 'I wish I had time.', col2: "I wish I'd had time." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 41, cat: 'if-wish',

    title: 'Wish',

    subtitle: 'All uses of "wish"',

    rules: [

      '"Wish + past simple" = present/future unreal wish.',

      '"Wish + past perfect" = past regret.',

      '"Wish + would" = wish for a change in behaviour (often frustration).',

      '"Wish" + to-infinitive (formal) = want: "I wish to speak to the manager."'

    ],

    formula: 'wish + past → now wish\nwish + past perfect → past regret\nwish + would → behaviour change wish',

    examples: [

      { text: "I <em>wish I lived</em> nearer the sea. (now wish)" },

      { text: "I <em>wish I had taken</em> that job. (past regret)" },

      { text: "I <em>wish you would stop</em> making that noise! (frustration)" },

      { text: "<em>I wish it would</em> stop raining." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "I wish I would have gone.", right: "I wish I had gone." }

    ]

  },

  // ── PASSIVE ──────────────────────────────────────────────────

  {

    id: 42, cat: 'passive',

    title: 'Passive 1 (is done / was done)',

    subtitle: 'Present and past passive',

    rules: [

      'Passive = <strong>be + past participle</strong>.',

      'Use when the <strong>action is more important than the doer</strong>, or the doer is unknown.',

      'The agent (who/what did it) is introduced by <em>by</em>.',

      'Present passive: am/is/are + done. Past passive: was/were + done.'

    ],

    formula: 'Active: Someone cleans the office.\nPassive: The office is cleaned (by someone).',

    examples: [

      { text: "My car <em>was stolen</em> last night." },

      { text: "These cars <em>are made</em> in Japan." },

      { text: "I wasn't informed. / Was anyone informed?" },

      { text: "The Eiffel Tower <em>was built</em> in 1889." },

      { text: "English <em>is spoken</em> all over the world." }

    ],

    notes: ['We often use the passive when we don\'t know who did the action, or when it\'s not important.'],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "The letter was sended.", right: "The letter was sent." },

      { wrong: "It was stole.", right: "It was stolen." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 43, cat: 'passive',

    title: 'Passive 2 (be done / been done / being done)',

    subtitle: 'Passive with infinitives and -ing',

    rules: [

      'Passive can appear with all tenses and modal verbs.',

      'After modals: <strong>modal + be + past participle</strong>.',

      'Continuous passive: <strong>is/was being done</strong>.',

      'Perfect passive: <strong>has/have/had been done</strong>.'

    ],

    formula: 'Modal passive: must/should/can + be done\nContinuous passive: is/was being done\nPerfect passive: has/have been done',

    examples: [

      { text: "The bridge <em>is being repaired</em>. (continuous passive)" },

      { text: "The house <em>has been painted</em>. (perfect passive)" },

      { text: "The application <em>must be submitted</em> by Friday." },

      { text: "He <em>was being followed</em>." },

      { text: "I don't like <em>being kept</em> waiting." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 44, cat: 'passive',

    title: 'Passive 3',

    subtitle: 'Get passive and it/there as passive subjects',

    rules: [

      '"Get" can replace "be" in the passive, especially in informal speech.',

      '"Get" passive often implies something happened suddenly or unexpectedly.',

      'Common "get" passive expressions: get hurt, get lost, get paid, get done.'

    ],

    formula: 'get + past participle → get passive (informal)',

    examples: [

      { text: "How did he <em>get hurt</em>?" },

      { text: "She <em>got promoted</em> last month." },

      { text: "I <em>got caught</em> in the rain." },

      { text: "The work <em>gets done</em> faster with two people." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 45, cat: 'passive',

    title: 'It is said that... / He is said to be...',

    subtitle: 'Passive reporting structures',

    rules: [

      'Verbs: <em>said, thought, believed, reported, considered, known, expected, supposed</em>.',

      'Two patterns: <strong>It is said that he is...</strong> OR <strong>He is said to be...</strong>',

      'For past: "He is said to have been..." (infinitive perfect).'

    ],

    formula: 'It is said/believed/reported + that-clause\nSubject + is said/believed/reported + to + infinitive',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>It is believed that</em> the suspect is hiding in France." },

      { text: "The suspect <em>is believed to be</em> hiding in France." },

      { text: "<em>It is said that</em> he runs ten miles a day." },

      { text: "He <em>is said to run</em> ten miles a day." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 46, cat: 'passive',

    title: 'Have something done',

    subtitle: 'Causative have/get',

    rules: [

      '"Have something done" = arrange for someone else to do it for you.',

      '"Get something done" = same meaning, more informal.',

      'Can also mean something happened to you (negative): "She had her bag stolen."'

    ],

    formula: 'have/get + object + past participle\nI had my hair cut. / I\'m getting my car fixed.',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>I had my hair cut</em> yesterday." },

      { text: "We're <em>having the house painted</em>." },

      { text: "She <em>had her bag stolen</em> on the train. (it happened to her)" },

      { text: "You should <em>get that tooth looked at</em>." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "I had cut my hair.", right: "I had my hair cut." }

    ]

  },

  // ── REPORTED SPEECH ──────────────────────────────────────────

  {

    id: 47, cat: 'reported',

    title: 'Reported speech 1 (He said that...)',

    subtitle: 'Reporting statements and tense shifts',

    rules: [

      'In reported speech, tenses typically <strong>shift back</strong>.',

      'present → past | present perfect → past perfect | will → would | can → could',

      '"Say" vs "Tell": say that / tell someone that.',

      'Time expressions shift: now → then, today → that day, yesterday → the day before.'

    ],

    formula: '"I\'m tired." → She said (that) she was tired.\n"I\'ll help." → He said he would help.',

    examples: [

      { text: "\"I <em>am</em> tired.\" → She said she <em>was</em> tired." },

      { text: "\"I <em>have finished</em>.\" → He said he <em>had finished</em>." },

      { text: "\"I <em>will come</em>.\" → She said she <em>would come</em>." },

      { text: "\"I <em>can swim</em>.\" → He said he <em>could swim</em>." }

    ],

    notes: ['Tense shift is not always necessary if the statement is still true: "She said she lives in London" (she still does).']

  },

  {

    id: 48, cat: 'reported',

    title: 'Reported speech 2',

    subtitle: 'Reporting questions and commands',

    rules: [

      'Reported questions: use normal word order (not question order), no question mark.',

      'Yes/no questions → reported with <em>if/whether</em>.',

      'Reported commands: <em>told/asked + someone + to + infinitive</em>.',

      '"Ask" and "tell" are both used with reported speech.'

    ],

    formula: 'Reported question: ask + subject + question word + subject + verb\nReported command: tell/ask + someone + to + base verb',

    examples: [

      { text: "\"Where do you live?\" → She asked me <em>where I lived</em>." },

      { text: "\"Are you coming?\" → He asked <em>if I was coming</em>." },

      { text: "\"Sit down!\" → She <em>told me to sit down</em>." },

      { text: "\"Please don't go.\" → He <em>asked her not to go</em>." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "She asked me where did I live.", right: "She asked me where I lived." }

    ]

  },

  // ── QUESTIONS & AUXILIARIES ──────────────────────────────────

  {

    id: 49, cat: 'questions',

    title: 'Questions 1',

    subtitle: 'Forming questions correctly',

    rules: [

      'Questions with auxiliary verbs (is/are, have, can, will etc.): <strong>auxiliary + subject + verb</strong>.',

      'Questions without auxiliary: use <strong>do/does/did</strong>.',

      'Question word as subject (who/what): NO auxiliary or do/does/did.',

      'Don\'t use do/does/did with be, have got, modals.'

    ],

    formula: 'Aux verb + subject + base verb?\nDo/does/did + subject + base verb?\nQuestion word as subject: Who + verb (no do)',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>Is she</em> coming? / <em>Can you</em> help?" },

      { text: "<em>What do you</em> do? / <em>Where did they</em> go?" },

      { text: "<em>Who told</em> you? (who = subject, no auxiliary needed)" },

      { text: "<em>What happened</em>? (not: What did happen?)" }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "What means this word?", right: "What does this word mean?" },

      { wrong: "Who did call you?", right: "Who called you?" }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 50, cat: 'questions',

    title: 'Questions 2 (Do you know where...?)',

    subtitle: 'Indirect questions',

    rules: [

      'Indirect questions have <strong>normal word order</strong> (not question word order).',

      'Begin with: <em>Do you know... / Can you tell me... / I wonder...</em>',

      'For yes/no indirect: use <em>if/whether</em>.',

      'Indirect questions = more polite than direct questions.'

    ],

    formula: 'Do you know + where/what/who + subject + verb?\nDo you know if + subject + verb?',

    examples: [

      { text: "Direct: Where is the station? → Indirect: <em>Do you know where the station is</em>?" },

      { text: "Direct: What time does it start? → <em>Can you tell me what time it starts</em>?" },

      { text: "<em>I wonder if</em> she is at home." },

      { text: "<em>Do you know whether</em> the bank is open?" }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "Do you know where is the bank?", right: "Do you know where the bank is?" }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 51, cat: 'questions',

    title: 'Auxiliary verbs / I think so / I hope so',

    subtitle: 'Short answers and responses',

    rules: [

      'Use auxiliary verbs in short answers to avoid repetition.',

      '"So do I / Neither do I" = agreement.',

      '"I think so / I hope so / I\'m afraid so" = positive short answer.',

      '"I don\'t think so / I hope not / I\'m afraid not" = negative short answer.'

    ],

    formula: 'I think so. / I don\'t think so.\nSo + auxiliary + subject. / Neither + auxiliary + subject.',

    examples: [

      { text: "'Are you tired?' 'Yes, <em>I am</em>.'" },

      { text: "'I love coffee.' '<em>So do I</em>!' / '<em>I don\'t.</em>'" },

      { text: "'Is it raining?' '<em>I think so</em>.' / '<em>I don\'t think so</em>.'" },

      { text: "'I can\'t swim.' '<em>Neither can I</em>.'" }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 52, cat: 'questions',

    title: 'Question tags',

    subtitle: 'Checking and confirming',

    rules: [

      'Positive sentence + negative tag. Negative sentence + positive tag.',

      'Tag uses the <strong>same auxiliary</strong> as the main clause.',

      'Rising intonation = genuine question. Falling = expecting agreement.',

      '"Aren\'t I?" (not: amn\'t I?) is the tag for "I am...".'

    ],

    formula: 'She\'s coming, isn\'t she? / He can\'t swim, can he?\nI\'m right, aren\'t I?',

    examples: [

      { text: "It's cold today, <em>isn't it</em>?" },

      { text: "You don't like it, <em>do you</em>?" },

      { text: "She hasn't called, <em>has she</em>?" },

      { text: "We should leave, <em>shouldn't we</em>?" }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "You like coffee, isn't it?", right: "You like coffee, don't you?" }

    ]

  },

  // ── -ING & INFINITIVES ───────────────────────────────────────

  {

    id: 53, cat: 'ing-inf',

    title: 'Verb + -ing (enjoy doing / stop doing etc.)',

    subtitle: 'Verbs followed by the -ing form',

    rules: [

      'These verbs are followed by <strong>-ing</strong> (not infinitive).',

      'Key verbs: <em>enjoy, mind, finish, stop, avoid, consider, recommend, suggest, miss, practise, keep, admit, deny, imagine, risk, fancy</em>.',

      '"Stop + -ing" = cease. "Stop + to-inf" = pause in order to do something.'

    ],

    formula: 'verb + -ing (no "to")\nI enjoy swimming. / She stopped smoking.',

    examples: [

      { text: "I <em>enjoy reading</em> in the evening." },

      { text: "Have you finished <em>doing</em> the dishes?" },

      { text: "<em>Stop complaining</em>!" },

      { text: "She <em>denied taking</em> the money." },

      { text: "I don't <em>mind waiting</em>." }

    ],

    notes: ['"Stop to smoke" = stopped what you were doing in order to smoke.\n"Stop smoking" = quit smoking.']

  },

  {

    id: 54, cat: 'ing-inf',

    title: 'Verb + to-infinitive (want to do / decide to do etc.)',

    subtitle: 'Verbs followed by the infinitive',

    rules: [

      'These verbs are followed by <strong>to + infinitive</strong>.',

      'Key verbs: <em>want, need, decide, plan, hope, expect, promise, offer, refuse, manage, agree, afford, attempt, fail, forget, learn, seem, appear, tend, pretend, claim</em>.',

      '"Seem/appear + to be" is very common.'

    ],

    formula: 'verb + to + base verb\nShe decided to leave. / He managed to pass.',

    examples: [

      { text: "I <em>want to go</em> home." },

      { text: "She <em>decided to stay</em>." },

      { text: "He <em>managed to open</em> the door." },

      { text: "They <em>offered to help</em>." },

      { text: "She <em>seems to be</em> happy." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "I want going home.", right: "I want to go home." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 55, cat: 'ing-inf',

    title: 'Verb + -ing or to-infinitive (remember/forget/stop etc.)',

    subtitle: 'Verbs that change meaning with -ing vs. to',

    rules: [

      '<strong>Remember + -ing</strong> = memory of past. <strong>Remember + to</strong> = don\'t forget to do it.',

      '<strong>Forget + -ing</strong> = forget a past experience. <strong>Forget + to</strong> = fail to do something.',

      '<strong>Try + -ing</strong> = experiment. <strong>Try + to</strong> = attempt.',

      '<strong>Regret + -ing</strong> = feel sorry about past. <strong>Regret + to</strong> = sorry to inform.'

    ],

    formula: 'Meaning changes with -ing vs. to-inf:\nremember/forget/try/regret + -ing OR to + base',

    examples: [

      { text: "I <em>remember meeting</em> her before. (memory of past event)" },

      { text: "<em>Remember to call</em> her! (don't forget — future)" },

      { text: "I <em>forgot locking</em> the door. (don't remember doing it)" },

      { text: "I <em>forgot to lock</em> the door. (didn't do it)" },

      { text: "<em>Try eating</em> less sugar. (experiment)" },

      { text: "I <em>tried to lift</em> it but it was too heavy. (attempted)" }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 56, cat: 'ing-inf',

    title: 'Verb + -ing or to-infinitive 2 (like/would like etc.)',

    subtitle: 'Like, love, hate, prefer',

    rules: [

      '"Like/love/hate + -ing" = general feeling about an activity.',

      '"Would like / would love + to" = specific wish (not general).',

      '"Would prefer + to" vs "prefer + -ing" (general preference).'

    ],

    formula: 'like/love/hate + -ing (general)\nwould like/love + to-inf (specific)',

    examples: [

      { text: "I <em>like swimming</em>. (generally)" },

      { text: "I <em>would like to swim</em> today. (specific desire)" },

      { text: "I <em>prefer cycling</em> to driving. (general preference)" },

      { text: "I <em>would prefer to take</em> the train. (specific preference)" }

    ],

    compare: [

      { col1: 'like + -ing (general)', col2: 'would like + to (specific)' },

      { col1: 'I like dancing.', col2: "I'd like to dance tonight." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 57, cat: 'ing-inf',

    title: 'Prefer and would rather',

    subtitle: 'Expressing preference',

    rules: [

      '"Prefer + -ing + to + -ing" = general preference.',

      '"Would prefer + to + inf" = specific preference.',

      '"Would rather + base verb" = preference (no "to").',

      '"Would rather + someone + past tense" = want someone else to do it.'

    ],

    formula: 'prefer + -ing to -ing\nwould rather + base verb (than)\nI\'d rather you did it (not I do it)',

    examples: [

      { text: "I <em>prefer cycling to walking</em>." },

      { text: "I <em>would rather walk</em> than take a bus." },

      { text: "She'd rather <em>you didn't tell</em> anyone." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 58, cat: 'ing-inf',

    title: 'Preposition + -ing',

    subtitle: '-ing after prepositions',

    rules: [

      'After a <strong>preposition</strong>, always use <strong>-ing</strong> (not infinitive).',

      'Common patterns: <em>interested in, good at, instead of, without, before, after, by, for</em>.',

      '"To" can be a preposition (look forward to doing, used to doing).'

    ],

    formula: 'preposition + verb-ing\nI\'m interested in learning. / She left without saying goodbye.',

    examples: [

      { text: "I'm <em>good at remembering</em> faces." },

      { text: "She left <em>without saying</em> goodbye." },

      { text: "<em>Before going</em> out, check the weather." },

      { text: "He lost weight <em>by eating</em> less." },

      { text: "I look forward <em>to hearing</em> from you." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "I look forward to hear from you.", right: "I look forward to hearing from you." },

      { wrong: "She left without to say goodbye.", right: "She left without saying goodbye." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 59, cat: 'ing-inf',

    title: 'Be/get used to... / used to...',

    subtitle: 'Familiarity and past habits',

    rules: [

      '"Used to + base verb" = past habit/state (no longer true).',

      '"Be used to + -ing" = be accustomed to something.',

      '"Get used to + -ing" = become accustomed to something.',

      '"Be/get used to" can be used in any tense; "used to" only in past.'

    ],

    formula: 'used to + base verb (past habit)\nbe/get used to + -ing (accustomed)',

    examples: [

      { text: "I <em>used to smoke</em> but I quit." },

      { text: "I'm <em>used to driving</em> on the left." },

      { text: "She's <em>getting used to</em> her new job." },

      { text: "He <em>was used to living</em> alone." }

    ],

    compare: [

      { col1: '"used to do"', col2: '"be used to doing"' },

      { col1: 'Past habit (now stopped)', col2: 'Accustomed to' },

      { col1: "I used to eat meat.", col2: "I'm used to eating alone." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 60, cat: 'ing-inf',

    title: 'Verb + object + to-infinitive',

    subtitle: 'Want/ask/tell/expect + object + to',

    rules: [

      'Some verbs take <strong>object + to-infinitive</strong>.',

      'Key verbs: <em>want, tell, ask, expect, help, need, would like, advise, remind, force, allow, enable</em>.',

      '"Let/make/help" take object + base verb (no "to"): let her go, made him work.'

    ],

    formula: 'verb + object + to + base verb\nI want you to come. / She told him to wait.',

    examples: [

      { text: "I <em>want you to help</em> me." },

      { text: "She <em>told me to be</em> quiet." },

      { text: "<em>Let her go</em>. (no to)" },

      { text: "He <em>made me laugh</em>. (no to)" },

      { text: "I <em>expect you to be</em> on time." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "She told me go.", right: "She told me to go." },

      { wrong: "He made me to cry.", right: "He made me cry." }

    ]

  },

  // ── ARTICLES & NOUNS ─────────────────────────────────────────

  {

    id: 61, cat: 'articles-nouns',

    title: 'Countable and uncountable nouns 1',

    subtitle: 'What can be counted?',

    rules: [

      '<strong>Countable</strong> nouns have singular and plural: a dog / dogs.',

      '<strong>Uncountable</strong> nouns have no plural: water, music, advice, information.',

      'With uncountable nouns: use <strong>some/any/much/a little</strong> — NOT a/an.',

      'Some nouns can be both: "Have a coffee" (cup) vs "I love coffee" (substance).'

    ],

    formula: 'Countable: a/an + noun | some + plural noun\nUncountable: some + noun (no a/an, no plural)',

    examples: [

      { text: "Do you have <em>any information</em>?", note: '(not: an information)' },

      { text: "Can I give you <em>some advice</em>?" },

      { text: "She gave me <em>a piece of advice</em>. (countable unit)" },

      { text: "I need <em>some water</em>." }

    ],

    notes: ['These are always uncountable in English: advice, information, news, knowledge, progress, research, evidence, luggage, furniture, traffic, weather.']

  },

  {

    id: 62, cat: 'articles-nouns',

    title: 'Countable and uncountable nouns 2',

    subtitle: 'Tricky uncountable nouns',

    rules: [

      'Words often treated as uncountable in English but countable in other languages.',

      '<em>Work, homework, bread, music, weather, scenery, trouble, damage, news</em>.',

      'Use "a + piece/item/bit + of" to make them countable.'

    ],

    formula: 'a piece of news/music/bread/advice/work\na bit of trouble',

    examples: [

      { text: "I have <em>a lot of work</em> to do.", note: '(not: many works)' },

      { text: "<em>The news</em> is surprising.", note: '(not: the news are)' },

      { text: "This is <em>lovely scenery</em>.", note: '(not: a lovely scenery)' }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 63, cat: 'articles-nouns',

    title: 'A/an and the',

    subtitle: 'The basic article rules',

    rules: [

      '"A/an" = introduce something for the <strong>first time</strong> or one of many.',

      '"The" = refer to something <strong>already known</strong> or unique.',

      '"A/an" = not specific. "The" = specific.',

      'Use "the" when there is only one (the sun, the moon, the president).'

    ],

    formula: 'First mention: a/an\nSecond mention / known: the',

    examples: [

      { text: "I saw <em>a dog</em> in the park. <em>The dog</em> was barking." },

      { text: "<em>The sun</em> is a star.", note: "(only one)" },

      { text: "She's <em>a</em> doctor.", note: "(one of many doctors)" },

      { text: "She's <em>the</em> doctor who treated me." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 64, cat: 'articles-nouns',

    title: 'The 1',

    subtitle: '"The" with specific reference',

    rules: [

      'Use "the" when it is <strong>clear which one</strong> you mean.',

      'Use "the" when something is <strong>unique</strong> in context.',

      'Use "the" when the noun is <strong>identified by a following phrase</strong>.',

      'Use "the" with superlatives and ordinal numbers.'

    ],

    formula: 'the + noun (when it\'s specific/clear/unique)',

    examples: [

      { text: "Can you close <em>the door</em>? (the one in this room)" },

      { text: "<em>The president</em> gave a speech. (unique role)" },

      { text: "<em>The book</em> I borrowed from you was great." },

      { text: "He's <em>the best</em> player on the team." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 65, cat: 'articles-nouns',

    title: 'The 2',

    subtitle: '"The" with groups and places',

    rules: [

      'Use "the" before <strong>adjectives used as nouns</strong>: the young, the elderly, the French.',

      'Use "the" with seas, oceans, rivers, mountain ranges, deserts.',

      'NO "the" with most countries, cities, mountains (single), lakes.',

      'Use "the" with plural country names: the USA, the Netherlands, the Philippines.'

    ],

    formula: 'the + adjective → group of people\nthe + geographical names (rivers, ranges, etc.)',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>The blind</em> need special assistance. (blind people)" },

      { text: "<em>The Nile</em>, <em>the Amazon</em>, <em>the Alps</em>." },

      { text: "I live in <em>France</em>. (not the France)" },

      { text: "I live in <em>the United States</em>." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 66, cat: 'articles-nouns',

    title: 'The 3',

    subtitle: '"The" with institutions and abstract nouns',

    rules: [

      'NO "the" with uncountable abstracts in general: love, life, happiness.',

      '"The" when specific: the life of Napoleon.',

      'NO "the" with: school, work, hospital, prison, church, university (when used for their main purpose).',

      '"The" when visiting as an outsider: the school (building).'

    ],

    formula: 'go to school (as student) / go to the school (as visitor)',

    examples: [

      { text: "She <em>went to hospital</em>. (as a patient)" },

      { text: "I <em>went to the hospital</em> to visit her. (as visitor)" },

      { text: "He <em>goes to school</em> by bus. (he's a student)" },

      { text: "<em>Life</em> is short. / <em>The life</em> of a soldier is hard." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 67, cat: 'articles-nouns',

    title: 'The 4 — general things',

    subtitle: 'Zero article for general meanings',

    rules: [

      'No article with <strong>plural countable nouns in general</strong>.',

      'No article with <strong>uncountable nouns in general</strong>.',

      '"The" makes things specific. No article = general.',

      '"Most + noun" (not the most).'

    ],

    formula: 'General: Dogs are friendly. / Music relaxes me.\nSpecific: The dogs in this park. / The music at the party.',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>Dogs</em> are loyal animals. (general)" },

      { text: "<em>The dogs</em> in that shelter need homes. (specific)" },

      { text: "<em>Crime</em> is increasing. (general)" },

      { text: "<em>Most people</em> prefer summer.", note: '(not the most people)' }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 68, cat: 'articles-nouns',

    title: 'A/an vs. the — typical mistakes',

    subtitle: 'Common article errors',

    rules: [

      'Use "a" when you mention something for the first time.',

      'Do NOT use "a/an" with uncountable or plural nouns in general.',

      'Do NOT use "the" for general meanings.',

      '"A" = one of many. "The" = the specific one.'

    ],

    examples: [

      { text: "She's <em>a</em> nurse. (profession = a/an)" },

      { text: "She's <em>the</em> nurse who helped me." },

      { text: "I need <em>a</em> doctor. / I called <em>the</em> doctor." },

      { text: "<em>The sun</em> rises in the east." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "I need an advice.", right: "I need some advice." },

      { wrong: "The life is beautiful.", right: "Life is beautiful. (general)" }

    ]

  },

  // ── PRONOUNS & DETERMINERS ───────────────────────────────────

  {

    id: 69, cat: 'pronouns-det',

    title: 'Some and any',

    subtitle: 'Positive and negative/question contexts',

    rules: [

      '"Some" = positive sentences and offers/requests.',

      '"Any" = questions and negative sentences.',

      '"Some" in questions = offering / expecting yes: "Would you like some coffee?"',

      '"Any" can mean "it doesn\'t matter which": "Any colour will do."'

    ],

    formula: 'some → positive context / offer\nany → question / negative',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>Some</em> children are playing outside." },

      { text: "Is there <em>any</em> bread left? / There isn't <em>any</em>." },

      { text: "Would you like <em>some</em> tea?", note: '(offer)' },

      { text: "You can come <em>any</em> day you like. (whichever)" }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 70, cat: 'pronouns-det',

    title: 'No / none / any',

    subtitle: 'Zero quantity',

    rules: [

      '"No" + noun: <em>No cars were parked here.</em>',

      '"None" = pronoun (no noun follows): <em>None of them came.</em>',

      '"Not any" = same as "no/none" but more emphatic.',

      '"None of" + plural noun can take singular or plural verb.'

    ],

    formula: 'no + noun | none (alone) | none of + the/my/us...',

    examples: [

      { text: "I have <em>no money</em>. / I have <em>none</em>." },

      { text: "<em>None of</em> the tickets were available." },

      { text: "<em>None</em> of my friends came." },

      { text: "There were <em>no</em> problems. / There weren't <em>any</em> problems." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 71, cat: 'pronouns-det',

    title: 'Much, many, little, few, a lot, plenty',

    subtitle: 'Quantity expressions',

    rules: [

      '"Much" + uncountable. "Many" + countable.',

      '"Little/a little" + uncountable. "Few/a few" + countable.',

      '"A little/a few" = some (positive). "Little/few" = not much/many (negative).',

      '"A lot of / plenty of" = large quantity (both countable and uncountable).'

    ],

    formula: 'much + uncount | many + count\na few + count | a little + uncount\nlots of / plenty of + both',

    examples: [

      { text: "There isn't <em>much</em> time. / There aren't <em>many</em> people." },

      { text: "I have <em>a few</em> friends in London. (some)" },

      { text: "<em>Few</em> people know the answer. (hardly any)" },

      { text: "She speaks <em>a little</em> French. (some)" },

      { text: "<em>Little</em> is known about his childhood. (hardly anything)" }

    ],

    compare: [

      { col1: '"a few" / "a little"', col2: '"few" / "little"' },

      { col1: 'Some (positive)', col2: 'Hardly any (negative)' },

      { col1: "I have a few ideas.", col2: "I have few ideas." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 72, cat: 'pronouns-det',

    title: 'All / all of / most / most of / no / none of',

    subtitle: 'Partitive structures',

    rules: [

      '"All/most/some/no" + noun (no "of"): <em>All children like games.</em>',

      '"All/most/some/none of" + the/my/this/us/them: <em>All of the children.</em>',

      '"All of us/them" but "all the students" (NOT "all of the students" in short form).',

      'With pronouns: must use "of": all of us, some of them.'

    ],

    formula: 'all/most + noun\nall/most of + the/my + noun or + pronoun',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>All children</em> need love. (general)" },

      { text: "<em>All of the children</em> passed. (specific group)" },

      { text: "<em>Most of my</em> friends are married." },

      { text: "<em>None of</em> the answers were correct." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 73, cat: 'pronouns-det',

    title: 'Both / both of / neither / neither of / either / either of',

    subtitle: 'Expressing two things',

    rules: [

      '"Both" = the two together (positive).',

      '"Neither" = not one and not the other.',

      '"Either" = one or the other (choice); also "not either" = neither.',

      '"Both/neither/either of" + the/my etc. or + us/them.'

    ],

    formula: 'both (plural verb) | neither/either (singular verb usually)',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>Both windows</em> were open." },

      { text: "<em>Neither answer</em> was correct." },

      { text: "<em>Either day</em> is fine with me." },

      { text: "<em>Both of them</em> came. / <em>Neither of them</em> came." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 74, cat: 'pronouns-det',

    title: 'All, every and whole',

    subtitle: 'Completeness and totality',

    rules: [

      '"All" + uncountable or plural: all the water / all students.',

      '"Every" + singular noun: every student (= all students individually).',

      '"Whole" = complete, nothing missing (before singular countable): the whole day.',

      '"All day" = the whole day. "Every day" = each day.'

    ],

    formula: 'whole + singular noun\nevery + singular noun\nall + plural / uncountable',

    examples: [

      { text: "I waited <em>all day</em>. / She ate <em>the whole cake</em>." },

      { text: "<em>Every student</em> passed the exam." },

      { text: "<em>All students</em> are welcome." },

      { text: "He spent <em>the whole summer</em> travelling." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 75, cat: 'pronouns-det',

    title: 'Each and every',

    subtitle: 'Individual vs. collective',

    rules: [

      '"Each" = individual items, one by one (often smaller groups).',

      '"Every" = all members of a group together.',

      '"Each" can be used as a pronoun: "They each have a key."',

      '"Each" of two or more. "Every" usually for three or more.'

    ],

    formula: 'each → individual focus\nevery → group / all',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>Each player</em> has a role." },

      { text: "<em>Every player</em> wants to win." },

      { text: "They gave <em>each</em> of us a present." },

      { text: "<em>Every time</em> I see him, he's busy." }

    ],

    compare: [

      { col1: '"Each"', col2: '"Every"' },

      { col1: 'One by one, individually', col2: 'All, collectively' },

      { col1: 'Each team played.', col2: 'Every team needs a coach.' }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 76, cat: 'pronouns-det',

    title: 'Myself / yourself / themselves etc.',

    subtitle: 'Reflexive pronouns',

    rules: [

      'Reflexive pronouns: <em>myself, yourself, himself, herself, itself, ourselves, yourselves, themselves</em>.',

      'Use when subject and object are the same.',

      '"By myself" = alone, without help.',

      'NOT used after: feel, relax, concentrate, meet, get up (unless emphasis needed).'

    ],

    formula: 'subject + verb + reflexive pronoun (same person)',

    examples: [

      { text: "She burnt <em>herself</em>." },

      { text: "He did it <em>himself</em>. (emphasis)" },

      { text: "They enjoyed <em>themselves</em> at the party." },

      { text: "I live <em>by myself</em>. (= alone)" }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "We enjoyed us.", right: "We enjoyed ourselves." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 77, cat: 'pronouns-det',

    title: 'A friend of mine / my own / on my own',

    subtitle: 'Double genitive and "own"',

    rules: [

      '"A friend of mine/yours/his" etc. = one of my friends.',

      '"My own" = emphasises personal possession / exclusivity.',

      '"On my own" = alone. "By myself" = without help.'

    ],

    formula: 'a/an + noun + of + mine/yours/his/hers/ours/theirs',

    examples: [

      { text: "She's <em>a friend of mine</em>. (not: a friend of me)" },

      { text: "This is <em>my own</em> recipe." },

      { text: "I live <em>on my own</em>. (alone)" },

      { text: "I did it <em>on my own</em>. (without help)" }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "She's a friend of me.", right: "She's a friend of mine." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 78, cat: 'pronouns-det',

    title: 'There... and it...',

    subtitle: 'Introductory subjects',

    rules: [

      '"There" introduces something <strong>new or unknown</strong>: There is a problem.',

      '"It" refers to something <strong>already known</strong> or to describe things.',

      '"There is/are/was/were/will be..."',

      '"It" is used for time, weather, distance, situations.'

    ],

    formula: 'there + be + new information\nit + be + description/reference',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>There is</em> a letter for you." },

      { text: "<em>There were</em> many people at the party." },

      { text: "<em>It</em> is cold today." },

      { text: "<em>It</em> takes 20 minutes to walk there." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "It is a problem.", right: "There is a problem. (introducing something new)" }

    ]

  },

  // ── RELATIVE CLAUSES ─────────────────────────────────────────

  {

    id: 79, cat: 'relative',

    title: 'Relative clauses 1 (who/that/which)',

    subtitle: 'Defining relative clauses',

    rules: [

      'Defining relative clause = identifies WHICH person/thing we mean.',

      '"Who/that" for people. "Which/that" for things.',

      'NO comma before a defining relative clause.',

      'The relative pronoun is the subject or object of the relative clause.'

    ],

    formula: 'noun + who/that/which + verb...',

    examples: [

      { text: "The man <em>who/that called</em> yesterday left a message." },

      { text: "The book <em>which/that I bought</em> was expensive." },

      { text: "She's the woman <em>who helped</em> me." },

      { text: "Is this the key <em>that you lost</em>?" }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 80, cat: 'relative',

    title: 'Relative clauses 2 (with and without who/that/which)',

    subtitle: 'Omitting the relative pronoun',

    rules: [

      'When the relative pronoun is the <strong>object</strong> of the clause, you can omit it.',

      'When it is the <strong>subject</strong>, you cannot omit it.',

      'Omitting is very common in spoken English.'

    ],

    formula: 'Object pronoun: can omit → "The book I read" (not "the book which I read")\nSubject pronoun: cannot omit → "The man who called" (not "the man called")',

    examples: [

      { text: "The film <em>(that) I saw</em> was brilliant." },

      { text: "The girl <em>(who) I met</em> was kind.", note: '(who = object)' },

      { text: "The man <em>who called</em> is here.", note: '(who = subject, cannot omit)' }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 81, cat: 'relative',

    title: 'Relative clauses 3 (whose/whom/where)',

    subtitle: 'More relative pronouns',

    rules: [

      '"Whose" = possessive (belonging to). "Whose book" = the book that belongs to...',

      '"Whom" = object form of "who" (formal). "The man whom I spoke to".',

      '"Where" = relative adverb for places: "the town where I was born".',

      '"When" = relative adverb for times: "the year when I left school".'

    ],

    formula: 'whose + noun\nwhere = in/at/to which\nwhen = at which time',

    examples: [

      { text: "That's the man <em>whose car</em> was stolen." },

      { text: "The person <em>to whom</em> I wrote hasn't replied. (formal)" },

      { text: "This is the house <em>where</em> I grew up." },

      { text: "2010 was the year <em>when</em> I got married." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 82, cat: 'relative',

    title: 'Relative clauses 4 — extra information (1)',

    subtitle: 'Non-defining relative clauses',

    rules: [

      'Non-defining clauses add <strong>extra information</strong> — they can be removed.',

      'Always use a <strong>comma</strong> before and after.',

      'Use <strong>who/which</strong> (NOT that).',

      'The relative pronoun cannot be omitted.'

    ],

    formula: 'noun, who/which + extra info, rest of sentence',

    examples: [

      { text: "My sister, <em>who lives in Rome</em>, is coming to visit." },

      { text: "The Eiffel Tower, <em>which was built in 1889</em>, is in Paris." },

      { text: "She lent me her car, <em>which was very kind of her</em>." }

    ],

    compare: [

      { col1: 'Defining (no comma)', col2: 'Non-defining (with comma)' },

      { col1: 'Identifies which one', col2: 'Adds extra info' },

      { col1: 'The man who called.', col2: 'Tom, who called yesterday...' }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 83, cat: 'relative',

    title: 'Relative clauses 5 — extra information (2)',

    subtitle: 'which and what in non-defining clauses',

    rules: [

      '"Which" can refer to the whole preceding clause.',

      '"What" is NOT used in relative clauses — use "what" in noun clauses.',

      'Non-defining: use "which" for things, "who" for people.'

    ],

    examples: [

      { text: "She passed the exam, <em>which surprised everyone</em>. (which = the whole fact)" },

      { text: "He was rude to me, <em>which upset me</em>." },

      { text: "I got the job, <em>which was brilliant news</em>." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 84, cat: 'relative',

    title: '-ing and -ed clauses',

    subtitle: 'Participle clauses reducing relative clauses',

    rules: [

      '"The man sitting in the corner" = The man who is sitting in the corner.',

      '"The car parked outside" = The car which is parked outside.',

      '-ing = active. -ed = passive.',

      'These are reduced relative clauses.'

    ],

    formula: 'verb-ing → active participle clause\nverb-ed → passive participle clause',

    examples: [

      { text: "The woman <em>talking to Tom</em> is his boss." },

      { text: "The boy <em>injured in the accident</em> is in hospital." },

      { text: "Most people <em>living in cities</em> prefer..." },

      { text: "The letter <em>sent last week</em> hasn't arrived." }

    ]

  },

  // ── ADJECTIVES & ADVERBS ─────────────────────────────────────

  {

    id: 85, cat: 'adj-adv',

    title: 'Adjectives ending in -ing and -ed',

    subtitle: 'Boring vs. bored, interesting vs. interested',

    rules: [

      '-ing adjective = describes a thing/situation.',

      '-ed adjective = describes how a person feels.',

      '"The film was boring." → The film causes boredom.',

      '"I was bored." → I felt boredom (because of the film).'

    ],

    formula: '-ing → causes the feeling (thing)\n-ed → experiences the feeling (person)',

    examples: [

      { text: "The lesson was <em>interesting</em>. / I was <em>interested</em>." },

      { text: "It was <em>shocking</em> news. / He was <em>shocked</em>." },

      { text: "The work is <em>tiring</em>. / She feels <em>tired</em>." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "I'm very boring at this party.", right: "I'm very bored at this party." },

      { wrong: "The film was very interested.", right: "The film was very interesting." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 86, cat: 'adj-adv',

    title: 'Adjectives: a nice new house / you look tired',

    subtitle: 'Order of adjectives',

    rules: [

      'Order of adjectives: opinion → size → age → shape → colour → origin → material.',

      '"Look/feel/smell/taste/seem/appear/become/get" are followed by adjectives (not adverbs).',

      'Predicative adjectives (after be/linking verb): she is happy, it looks good.'

    ],

    formula: 'Opinion > Size > Age > Shape > Colour > Origin > Material\na lovely big old round red Spanish wooden box',

    examples: [

      { text: "A <em>nice new Italian</em> restaurant." },

      { text: "A <em>big old red</em> car." },

      { text: "You <em>look tired</em>.", note: '(not: tiredly)' },

      { text: "This soup <em>tastes good</em>." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "A red beautiful car.", right: "A beautiful red car. (opinion before colour)" }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 87, cat: 'adj-adv',

    title: 'Adjectives and adverbs 1 (quick/quickly)',

    subtitle: 'Adjectives modify nouns; adverbs modify verbs',

    rules: [

      'Adjectives modify nouns. Adverbs modify verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs.',

      'Most adverbs: adjective + <strong>-ly</strong>.',

      'Some adjectives end in -ly: friendly, lovely, lonely, lively (not adverbs!).',

      '"Good" = adjective. "Well" = adverb. But "well" can also be an adjective meaning healthy.'

    ],

    formula: 'adj + noun → a quick answer\nverb + adverb → She answered quickly.',

    examples: [

      { text: "He's a <em>careful</em> driver. / He drives <em>carefully</em>." },

      { text: "She's <em>fluent</em> in French. / She speaks French <em>fluently</em>." },

      { text: "That's a <em>good</em> idea. / You speak English very <em>well</em>." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "She speaks English good.", right: "She speaks English well." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 88, cat: 'adj-adv',

    title: 'Adjectives and adverbs 2 (well/fast/late, hard/hardly)',

    subtitle: 'Irregular adverbs',

    rules: [

      'Some adverbs have the same form as the adjective: fast, hard, late, early, high, low.',

      '"Hard" (adjective/adverb) vs "hardly" (= almost not).',

      '"Late" vs "lately" (= recently).',

      '"High" vs "highly" (= very/greatly).',

      '"Near" vs "nearly" (= almost).'

    ],

    formula: 'hard (adverb) ≠ hardly (= almost not)\nlate (adverb) ≠ lately (= recently)',

    examples: [

      { text: "She works <em>hard</em>. / She <em>hardly works</em>. (almost doesn't)" },

      { text: "He arrived <em>late</em>. / I haven't seen him <em>lately</em>." },

      { text: "The bird flew <em>high</em>. / I <em>highly recommend</em> it." }

    ],

    compare: [

      { col1: 'Same form', col2: '-ly form (different meaning)' },

      { col1: 'She works hard.', col2: "She hardly works. (≈ doesn't work)" },

      { col1: 'He came late.', col2: "I haven't seen him lately." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 89, cat: 'adj-adv',

    title: 'So and such',

    subtitle: 'Expressing degree and emphasis',

    rules: [

      '"So" + adjective/adverb: <em>so beautiful, so quickly</em>.',

      '"Such" + (a/an) + noun phrase: <em>such a beautiful day, such nice people</em>.',

      '"So/such ... that" = result clause.',

      '"Such a" + singular countable. "Such" + uncountable/plural (no a/an).'

    ],

    formula: 'so + adj/adv → so cold\nsuch + (a) + noun → such a cold day',

    examples: [

      { text: "It was <em>so cold</em> that we stayed inside." },

      { text: "It was <em>such a cold day</em> that we stayed inside." },

      { text: "She's <em>such a</em> kind person." },

      { text: "They were <em>such</em> nice people." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "It was such cold.", right: "It was so cold. (so + adj)" },

      { wrong: "It was so a cold day.", right: "It was such a cold day." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 90, cat: 'adj-adv',

    title: 'Enough and too',

    subtitle: '"Too much" and "not enough"',

    rules: [

      '"Too" = more than necessary/desirable (negative).',

      '"Enough" = the right amount or sufficient.',

      '"Too + adj/adv" + (for + object) + (to + inf).',

      '"Adj/adv + enough" (enough comes AFTER adj/adv). "Enough + noun" (before noun).'

    ],

    formula: 'too + adj/adv\nadj/adv + enough\nenough + noun',

    examples: [

      { text: "This coffee is <em>too hot</em> to drink." },

      { text: "She wasn't <em>old enough</em> to see the film." },

      { text: "I don't have <em>enough money</em>." },

      { text: "The hotel was <em>big enough for</em> all of us." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "She is enough old.", right: "She is old enough." },

      { wrong: "I have enough of money.", right: "I have enough money." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 91, cat: 'adj-adv',

    title: 'Quite, pretty, rather and fairly',

    subtitle: 'Degree adverbs',

    rules: [

      '"Fairly" = to some degree (less strong).',

      '"Quite" = less than very / moderately.',

      '"Rather" = stronger than quite; often used with negative ideas.',

      '"Pretty" = quite / fairly (informal).',

      '"Quite" before a/an: "quite a good film" (not "a quite good film").'

    ],

    formula: 'Scale: fairly < quite < rather / pretty\nquite a + noun (not a quite + noun)',

    examples: [

      { text: "The hotel was <em>fairly</em> good.", note: '(adequate)' },

      { text: "The film was <em>quite</em> interesting." },

      { text: "The weather was <em>rather</em> cold.", note: '(stronger complaint)' },

      { text: "It's <em>quite a</em> long way from here." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 92, cat: 'adj-adv',

    title: 'Comparison 1 (cheaper, more expensive)',

    subtitle: 'Comparative adjectives and adverbs',

    rules: [

      'Short adjectives (1–2 syllables): add <strong>-er</strong>.',

      'Long adjectives (3+ syllables): use <strong>more</strong>.',

      'Irregular: good → better, bad → worse, far → farther/further.',

      'Comparison: use <strong>than</strong> after comparative.'

    ],

    formula: 'adj + -er than\nmore + adj + than\nless + adj + than',

    examples: [

      { text: "This car is <em>cheaper than</em> that one." },

      { text: "She's <em>more intelligent than</em> her brother." },

      { text: "I feel <em>better</em> today than yesterday." },

      { text: "This road is <em>less busy than</em> the other." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 93, cat: 'adj-adv',

    title: 'Comparison 2 (much better / the sooner the better)',

    subtitle: 'Strengthening comparatives',

    rules: [

      'Strengthen comparatives with: <em>much, far, a lot, a bit, a little, slightly</em>.',

      '"Any better" = used in negatives and questions.',

      '"Better and better" = progressive comparison (getting progressively so).',

      '"The + comparative, the + comparative" = the more you do X, the more Y.'

    ],

    formula: 'much/far/a lot + comparative\nthe + comparative + the + comparative',

    examples: [

      { text: "He's <em>much better</em> now." },

      { text: "Your English is getting <em>better and better</em>." },

      { text: "<em>The sooner the better</em>." },

      { text: "<em>The more I practise, the more confident</em> I become." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 94, cat: 'adj-adv',

    title: 'Comparison 3 (as...as / than)',

    subtitle: 'Equality and inequality',

    rules: [

      '"As + adj/adv + as" = equal comparison.',

      '"Not as...as" = one is less than the other.',

      '"Twice as...as" / "three times as...as" = multiple comparison.',

      '"The same as" = exactly equal.'

    ],

    formula: 'as + adj/adv + as → equal\nnot as + adj/adv + as → less',

    examples: [

      { text: "She's <em>as tall as</em> her brother." },

      { text: "This job isn't <em>as well paid as</em> my last one." },

      { text: "It costs <em>twice as much as</em> last year." },

      { text: "My car is <em>the same age as</em> yours." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 95, cat: 'adj-adv',

    title: 'Superlatives (the longest, the most enjoyable)',

    subtitle: 'Comparing three or more',

    rules: [

      'Short adjectives: <strong>the + -est</strong>.',

      'Long adjectives: <strong>the most + adj</strong>.',

      'Always use "the" before superlatives.',

      'Use <em>ever, of all, in the world</em> with superlatives.',

      'Irregular: good → the best, bad → the worst, far → the furthest.'

    ],

    formula: 'the + adj-est | the most + adj',

    examples: [

      { text: "This is <em>the longest</em> river in the world." },

      { text: "She's <em>the most talented</em> student in the class." },

      { text: "That was <em>the worst</em> meal I've <em>ever</em> had." },

      { text: "It's <em>the best</em> film of all time." }

    ]

  },

  // ── CONJUNCTIONS & PREPOSITIONS ──────────────────────────────

  {

    id: 96, cat: 'conj-prep',

    title: 'Although / though / even though / in spite of / despite',

    subtitle: 'Contrast and concession',

    rules: [

      '"Although/though/even though" = conjunction + clause.',

      '"In spite of / despite" = preposition + noun/-ing.',

      '"Even though" is stronger than "although".',

      '"Though" can go at the end (informal): "I still liked it, though."'

    ],

    formula: 'although/though/even though + clause\nin spite of / despite + noun/-ing',

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>Although</em> it was raining, we went out." },

      { text: "<em>In spite of</em> the rain, we went out." },

      { text: "<em>Despite being</em> tired, she finished the work." },

      { text: "I liked it, <em>though</em>.", note: '(end position — informal)' }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "Despite it was cold, we went out.", right: "Despite the cold, / Although it was cold, we went out." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 97, cat: 'conj-prep',

    title: 'In case',

    subtitle: 'Precaution',

    rules: [

      '"In case" = as a precaution (you do something NOW in case something happens LATER).',

      '"In case" is NOT the same as "if".',

      '"Just in case" = for extra safety.',

      'After "in case": present (not will) or past.'

    ],

    formula: 'action (now) + in case + possible situation (later)\nI take an umbrella in case it rains.',

    examples: [

      { text: "Take an umbrella <em>in case</em> it rains. (precaution)" },

      { text: "I wrote it down <em>in case</em> I forgot." },

      { text: "I'll take some cash <em>in case</em> the card doesn't work." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "Take an umbrella in case it will rain.", right: "Take an umbrella in case it rains." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 98, cat: 'conj-prep',

    title: 'Unless / as long as / provided',

    subtitle: 'Conditions',

    rules: [

      '"Unless" = if not: "I won\'t go unless you come." = "I won\'t go if you don\'t come."',

      '"As long as / so long as" = on condition that.',

      '"Provided/providing" = if; given that (formal condition).',

      'All followed by present simple for future conditions.'

    ],

    formula: 'unless = if not\nas long as / provided + condition → result',

    examples: [

      { text: "I'll come <em>unless</em> it rains. (= if it doesn't rain)" },

      { text: "You can borrow the car <em>as long as</em> you're careful." },

      { text: "<em>Provided you study</em>, you'll pass." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 99, cat: 'conj-prep',

    title: 'As (as I walked along... / as I was hungry)',

    subtitle: 'Multiple meanings of "as"',

    rules: [

      '"As" = at the same time as (temporal).',

      '"As" = because / since (reason).',

      '"As" = in the way that.',

      '"As" ≠ "like" (as = conjunction + clause; like = preposition + noun).'

    ],

    formula: 'as (temporal) = while\nas (reason) = because\nas (manner) = in the way that',

    examples: [

      { text: "I saw her <em>as</em> I was leaving. (while)" },

      { text: "<em>As</em> I was tired, I went to bed early. (because)" },

      { text: "Do it <em>as</em> I showed you. (in the way that)" }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 100, cat: 'conj-prep',

    title: 'Like and as',

    subtitle: 'Comparison and function',

    rules: [

      '"Like" = similar to (preposition + noun/pronoun).',

      '"As" = in the function of (preposition + noun in role).',

      '"As" = conjunction (before clause).',

      'Common phrase: "like me / like she does" — "like" is informal here, "as" is formal.'

    ],

    formula: 'like + noun/pronoun (similarity)\nas + noun (function/role)\nas + clause (conjunction)',

    examples: [

      { text: "She swims <em>like a fish</em>." },

      { text: "He works <em>as a teacher</em>. (he is a teacher)" },

      { text: "He works <em>like a teacher</em>. (not a teacher, but similar style)" },

      { text: "<em>As I said</em>, the meeting is tomorrow." }

    ],

    compare: [

      { col1: '"Like"', col2: '"As"' },

      { col1: 'Similarity', col2: 'Function / identity' },

      { col1: "He drinks like a fish.", col2: "She works as a nurse." }

    ]

  },

  // ── PREPOSITIONS ─────────────────────────────────────────────

  {

    id: 101, cat: 'prepositions',

    title: 'At/on/in (time)',

    subtitle: 'Time prepositions',

    rules: [

      '"At" = a precise time: <em>at 3 o\'clock, at midnight, at noon, at the weekend</em>.',

      '"On" = days and dates: <em>on Monday, on 5 June, on Christmas Day</em>.',

      '"In" = longer periods: <em>in January, in 2020, in the morning, in summer</em>.',

      'No preposition before: next/last/this/every + time word.'

    ],

    formula: 'at (point) | on (day) | in (period)',

    examples: [

      { text: "The meeting is <em>at</em> 3pm / <em>on</em> Monday / <em>in</em> March." },

      { text: "I'll see you <em>at</em> Christmas / <em>on</em> New Year's Day." },

      { text: "She's coming <em>next week</em>.", note: '(no preposition)' },

      { text: "<em>In</em> the morning / <em>at</em> night." }

    ],

    mistakes: [

      { wrong: "She arrived in Monday.", right: "She arrived on Monday." },

      { wrong: "We met at 2019.", right: "We met in 2019." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 102, cat: 'prepositions',

    title: 'In/at/on (position) 1',

    subtitle: 'Place prepositions — basic uses',

    rules: [

      '"In" = inside a space, area, or country.',

      '"At" = a specific point or location (at the bus stop, at the airport).',

      '"On" = surface contact.',

      '"In" or "at" for places can depend on how we view the place (as an area vs a point).'

    ],

    formula: 'in → enclosed space / area\nat → specific point / location\non → surface',

    examples: [

      { text: "She's <em>in</em> the kitchen / <em>in</em> London / <em>in</em> the car." },

      { text: "I'll meet you <em>at</em> the airport." },

      { text: "The book is <em>on</em> the table." },

      { text: "There are clouds <em>in</em> the sky." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 103, cat: 'prepositions',

    title: 'In/at/on (position) 2 and 3',

    subtitle: 'Advanced position uses',

    rules: [

      '"In" with seas, rivers, areas, streets (walking in the street).',

      '"On" with floors (on the first floor), roads (on the A40).',

      '"At" with addresses, events, buildings used for activities.',

      'On/in with transport: on a bus/train/plane; in a car/taxi.'

    ],

    examples: [

      { text: "She lives <em>in</em> Oxford Street. / She's <em>on</em> Oxford Street." },

      { text: "My office is <em>on</em> the third floor." },

      { text: "I met him <em>at</em> a conference." },

      { text: "We travelled <em>on</em> the train / <em>in</em> a taxi." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 104, cat: 'prepositions',

    title: 'By',

    subtitle: 'Many uses of "by"',

    rules: [

      '"By" = no later than (deadline): by Friday = before or on Friday.',

      '"By" = passive agent: written by Shakespeare.',

      '"By" = means of transport/communication: by bus, by email.',

      '"By" = method: by hand, by chance, by mistake.'

    ],

    formula: 'by + time = deadline\nby + person/thing = agent\nby + transport = how you travel',

    examples: [

      { text: "Can you finish it <em>by</em> Monday?" },

      { text: "The play was written <em>by</em> Wilde." },

      { text: "I usually go to work <em>by</em> train." },

      { text: "I broke it <em>by</em> mistake." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 105, cat: 'prepositions',

    title: 'Noun + preposition (reason for, cause of)',

    subtitle: 'Fixed noun + preposition combinations',

    rules: [

      'Many nouns have fixed prepositions that follow them.',

      'Key combinations: <em>reason for, cause of, demand for, increase in, solution to, attitude towards, advantage of, benefit of</em>.'

    ],

    formula: 'noun + fixed preposition + noun/-ing',

    examples: [

      { text: "I don't know the <em>reason for</em> his decision." },

      { text: "There was a sudden <em>increase in</em> prices." },

      { text: "There is no easy <em>solution to</em> this problem." },

      { text: "What's your <em>attitude towards</em> nuclear energy?" }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 106, cat: 'prepositions',

    title: 'Adjective + preposition',

    subtitle: 'Fixed adjective + preposition combinations',

    rules: [

      'Many adjectives are followed by fixed prepositions.',

      'Key combinations with "of": afraid of, aware of, capable of, fond of, proud of, sure of, tired of.',

      'Key combinations with "at": good at, bad at, surprised at, shocked at.',

      'Key combinations with "about": excited about, worried about, angry about, happy about.'

    ],

    formula: 'adjective + fixed preposition',

    examples: [

      { text: "I'm <em>good at</em> maths. / She's <em>bad at</em> cooking." },

      { text: "They were <em>surprised at</em> the result." },

      { text: "She's <em>worried about</em> her exam." },

      { text: "He's <em>proud of</em> his children." },

      { text: "I'm <em>fond of</em> dark chocolate." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 107, cat: 'prepositions',

    title: 'Verb + preposition',

    subtitle: 'Verbs that require specific prepositions',

    rules: [

      'Many verbs are followed by fixed prepositions.',

      'Common verb + preposition patterns.',

      'The preposition is always followed by a noun or -ing form.'

    ],

    formula: 'verb + preposition + noun/-ing',

    examples: [

      { text: "I <em>believe in</em> second chances." },

      { text: "She <em>apologised for</em> being late." },

      { text: "Can you <em>look after</em> my cat?" },

      { text: "We <em>arrived at</em> the airport on time." },

      { text: "He <em>insisted on</em> paying the bill." },

      { text: "I <em>agree with</em> your decision." }

    ]

  },

  // ── PHRASAL VERBS ────────────────────────────────────────────

  {

    id: 108, cat: 'phrasal',

    title: 'Phrasal verbs 1 — General points',

    subtitle: 'What are phrasal verbs?',

    rules: [

      'A phrasal verb = verb + particle (in/out/on/off/up/down/away/back).',

      'The particle can change the meaning completely.',

      'Separable: the object can go between verb and particle (turn off the TV / turn the TV off).',

      'Inseparable: object must follow the particle (look after the baby — NOT look the baby after).',

      'With pronouns: always between verb and particle (turn it off — NOT turn off it).'

    ],

    formula: 'verb + particle (separable or inseparable)\nWith pronoun: verb + pronoun + particle',

    examples: [

      { text: "Can you <em>turn off</em> the TV? / Can you <em>turn</em> the TV <em>off</em>?" },

      { text: "<em>Turn it off</em>. (pronoun — must separate)" },

      { text: "She <em>looked after</em> the children. (inseparable)" }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 109, cat: 'phrasal',

    title: 'Phrasal verbs 2 — in/out',

    subtitle: '"In" and "out" as particles',

    rules: [

      '"In" often suggests entering or involvement.',

      '"Out" often suggests exiting or completion.',

      'Common phrasal verbs with in: come in, fill in, give in, break in.',

      'Common phrasal verbs with out: find out, work out, run out of, go out, put out.'

    ],

    examples: [

      { text: "<em>Come in</em>! / <em>Fill in</em> the form." },

      { text: "I <em>found out</em> the truth." },

      { text: "We've <em>run out of</em> milk." },

      { text: "She <em>worked out</em> the answer." },

      { text: "The fire was <em>put out</em>." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 110, cat: 'phrasal',

    title: 'Phrasal verbs 3 — out',

    subtitle: 'Completing and exhausting',

    rules: [

      '"Out" can mean: completely (wear out), to the end (work out), distributing (give out).',

      'Common phrasal verbs: carry out, leave out, let out, point out, sort out, wear out.'

    ],

    examples: [

      { text: "The project was <em>carried out</em> successfully." },

      { text: "Don't <em>leave out</em> the important details." },

      { text: "She <em>pointed out</em> my mistakes." },

      { text: "My shoes are completely <em>worn out</em>." }

    ]

  },

  {

    id: 111, cat: 'phrasal',

    title: 'Phrasal verbs 4–5 — on/off',

    subtitle: 'Continuing and stopping',

    rules: [

      '"On" often = continuing, attached, forward.',

      '"Off" often = departing, detaching, stopping.',

      '"Put on" vs "put off": put on clothes vs postpone.',

      '"Go on" = continue. "Call off" = cancel.'

    ],

    examples: [
